coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified La2Ti2O7 nanosheets for visible light photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zheng
Wu, Limin
Wang, Peng
Zhang, Yuexing
Wan, Shuai
Guo, Xiaoliang
Jin, Weiyang
Zhang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: To overcome the drawback of UV light response in La2Ti2O7 nanosheets, novel up-conversion carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with visible light response have been introduced on the surface of La2Ti2O7 for efficient enhancement of photocatalytic performance. The resulted CQDs/La2Ti2O7 samples were characterized by XRD, TEM, FT-IR, XPS and UV–vis, respectively. Rhodamine B (RhB) solution was chosen as the target organic pollutant to evaluate the photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation. The results indicated that 5 wt% loading of CQDs composite exhibited the best performance. Both the up-converted photoluminescence behavior and their electron capturing ability of CQDs are considered as the main reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic abilities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: La2Ti2O7
Photocatalysis
Carbon quantum dots
Semiconductors
Nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL spectra of CQDs with different wavelength excitation.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) HRTEM image of CQDs/La2Ti2O7, (c) FT-IR spectra of three samples, and (d) XPS spectra of composites for C 1s and full survey (inset).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) photocatalytic activity, (c) trapping experiments, and (d) schematic illustration for the photocatalytic process.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified La2Ti2O7 nanosheets for visible light photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering and Hebei Key Laboratory of Boron Nitride Micro and Nano Materials, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering and Hebei Key Laboratory of Boron Nitride Micro and Nano Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To overcome the drawback of UV light response in La2Ti2O7 nanosheets, novel up-conversion carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with visible light response have been introduced on the surface of La2Ti2O7 for efficient enhancement of photocatalytic performance. The resulted CQDs/La2Ti2O7 samples were characterized by XRD, TEM, FT-IR, XPS and UV–vis, respectively. Rhodamine B (RhB) solution was chosen as the target organic pollutant to evaluate the photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation. The results indicated that 5 wt% loading of CQDs composite exhibited the best performance. Both the up-converted photoluminescence behavior and their electron capturing ability of CQDs are considered as the main reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic abilities.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor-based photocatalysis technology has been regarded as one of the most appealing methods for solving energy and pollution problems due to its economical and ecological safety. TiO2-based materials for photocatalytic applications have been studied for long times, moreover, ternary and other complex oxide systems have been increasingly explored as photocatalysts [1]. Of these materials, layered lanthanum titanate (La2Ti2O7) has been widely explored due to its high photocatalytic properties in water splitting and pollutants decomposition [2]. However, its wide band gap (about 3.4 eV) limits the utilization under the solar spectrum. Now, lots of strategies have been adopted to enhance the visible photocatalytic activity of La2Ti2O7, such as morphological controlling, doping and heterojunction constructions [3–5].

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high aqueous solubility, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity and good photoinduced electron transfer ability, have been successfully incorporated into semiconductor photocatalysts to enhance the visible light activity [6–9]. Notably, the unique up-converted photoluminescence (PL) behavior of CQDs offers the ability to convert visible light to shorter wavelength to excite the wide band-gap semiconductor to enhance the photocatalytic activity. Inspired by these analyses, it is reasonable to expect that the La2Ti2O7 nanosheets coupled with CQDs may show superior visible-light photocatalytic activity, which has not been well explored.

Herein, we present CQDs/ La2Ti2O7 nanosheet composites with excellent visible-light photocatalytic activity via a simple route. And the optimal loading amount of CQDs has been identified to be 5 wt%. Further, the reasonable enhancement mechanism of the visible light photocatalysis has been studied.

CQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal reactions of vitamin C in water and ethanol solution at 180 °C for 5 h [10]. Then, the solution was subjected to dialysis for 24 h to remove all impurity molecules. The CQDs were dispersed into water to obtain a suspension (1 mg/ml) for further use. La2Ti2O7 nanosheets were synthesized via the hydrothermal reactions of La(NO3)3·6H2O and Ti(SO4)2 in 1 mol/L NaOH aqueous solution at 200 °C for 24 h [11].

0.1 g of La2Ti2O7 nanosheets was dispersed in 50 ml of ethanol and sonicated for 2 h. Then, certain volume of CQDs solution was added into the solution and stirred for 2 h, and dried at 60 °C overnight. CQDs/La2Ti2O7 composites prepared by changing the amount of CQDs solution of 2.5, 5.0, 7.5 and 10 ml were labeled as C-LTO-2.5, C-LTO-5, C-LTO-7.5 and C-LTO-10, respectively.

The XRD was performed on BRUKER D8 FOCUS diffractometer. The morphology and structure were characterized using SEM (HITACHI S-4800) and TEM Tecnai F20 electron microscope (Philips, Netherlands). Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were recorded on a HITACHI F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. FTIR spectra were examined with a Perkin-Elmer Frontier FT-IR/FIR Spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on an ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer. UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were recorded using a spectrophotometer (HITACHI, U-3900H).

A 300 W Xe light (CEL-HXUV300, Zhongjiao Aulight Science & Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing, China) with an ultraviolet cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm) was used as the visible light source (420–800 nm). Typically, 0.1 g of powder was dispersed into 100 ml of RhB solution (10 mg/L). Visible light illumination was conducted after the suspension was strongly magnetically stirred in the dark for 1 h to reach the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. During irradiation, ∼5 ml of the suspension continually was taken from the reaction cell at given time intervals and analyzed by the spectrophotometer.

Fig. 1 is the up-converted PL spectra of CQDs measured by using excitation wavelengths ranging from 600 to 800 nm with the interval of 25 nm. It is obvious that the up-converted emission exhibits an excitation-dependent behavior and the emission wavelengths are located in the range from 330 to 450 nm, which is corresponded to previously report [10]. Therefore, the La2Ti2O7 nanosheets with wide band gap about 3.4 eV can be excited by the emission light from CQDs [4].

Fig. 2 a is the XRD pattern of La2Ti2O7 nanosheets with different amounts of CQDs. It clearly demonstrates that no any new peaks can be observed after the introduction of CQDs. This may be due to the small amount and low crystallinity of CQDs in the composites, as well as the good dispersion of CQDs on the nanosheets [12]. Fig. 2b shows the HRTEM image of CQDs/La2Ti2O7 composites. The well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.323 nm indexed to the (0 0 2) plane of CQDs [13]. The good interfacial contact between CQDs and La2Ti2O7 nanosheet is highly expected to improve the separation of photo-induced electrons and holes.

The FT-IR spectra of CQDs, La2Ti2O7 nanosheets and C-LTO-5 are shown in Fig. 2c. For CQDs/La2Ti2O7 composites, both the partial absorption peaks of CQDs and the typical absorption peaks of La2Ti2O7 are observed. We note that the intensity becomes weaker than single component and there is a red shift for absorption band below 2000 cm−1 compared with that of CQDs, both of which indicating that CQDs is successfully introduced onto La2Ti2O7 [13]. In addition, XPS was also conducted to prove the existence of CQDs in the composites. The full survey spectrum (inset in Fig. 2d) shows the presence of La 3d, Ti 2p, O 1s, and C 1s in the CQDs/La2Ti2O7 composite. In the narrow scan of the C 1s (Fig. 2d), the deconvoluted peaks at 284.6, 286.1, and 288.6 eV are attributed to the CC, CO and CO bonds, respectively, suggesting the presence of the CQDs in the composite [14].

UV–vis spectrum of La2Ti2O7 and several CQDs/La2Ti2O7 composites with different mass ratios is displayed in Fig. 3 a. Obviously, no visible light absorption for La2Ti2O7 nanosheets can be observed in the region of 400–800 nm. While all the CQDs/La2Ti2O7 composite samples exhibit obvious absorption in visible region, and the absorption increases gradually with the increase of CQDs contents. Therefore, the enhanced visible light absorption of the CQDs/La2Ti2O7 endows them with possible visible-light responsive activities.

The photocatalytic activities of as-prepared samples were evaluated by the RhB degradation under visible light illumination. As shown in Fig. 3b, the degradation efficiency of CQDs/La2Ti2O7 composites is significantly higher than that of the controlled pure La2Ti2O7 nanosheets due to the excellent visible light absorption abilities for composites. Among them, the C-LTO-5 sample with 5 wt% CQDs loading exhibits the best photocatalytic efficiency, where 99% of RhB can be degraded after 90 min of illumination. As seen, the photocatalytic efficiency enhances gradually with the increasing CQDs loading amounts, which may be ascribe to the corresponding improvement of visible light absorption intensity. However, further increasing the amount of CQDs leads an obvious efficiency decrease due to the competition for visible light absorption and photocatalytic inactivity of CQDs. Such phenomenon can also be observed in other CQDs photocatalytic system [12–14].

The photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/La2Ti2O7 nanosheet composites was investigated by performing the radical-trapping experiments with different scavengers. ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), isopropanol (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ) were employed as scavengers for holes (h+), hydroxyl (·OH) and superoxide (O2
−) radicals, respectively [4]. Fig. 3c shows the results after adding 1 mM different scavengers. The addition of IPA had a little influence for photocatalytic activity, implying that hydroxyl radical was not the dominant active species. However, it was significantly suppressed when EDTA and benzoquinone were introduced, which indicates that both h+ and O2
− radicals are the main active species during RhB photodegradation, and the superoxide plays the most important role in the system.

Based on the above experimental results, we propose a possible mechanism to explain the visible light activation for pure La2Ti2O7 by introducing CQDs. CQDs can convert visible light and/or near-infra-red light to shorter wavelengths due to the up-converted property. The shorter wavelengths that less than or equal to 365 nm can excite La2Ti2O7 nanosheets to generate electrons and holes. At the same time, CQDs can accept the photoinduced electrons from the conduction band of La2Ti2O7, which promotes the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [15]. Then, O2
− radicals formed on the surface of CQDs with dissolved oxygen and holes in the valance band of La2Ti2O7 could directly oxidize the RhB dye molecules together.

In summary, we focus on the visible light activation of La2Ti2O7 nanosheets by taking advantage of up-conversion properties of CQDs. The synthesized CQDs were identified to possess obvious up-conversion properties that could transfer visible light to UV light. The photocatalytic performance of the composites is dependent on its content of CQDs, in which sample with 5 wt% loading possesses the best performance. The quenching experiment indicated the O2
− and holes radicals were the major active species in the photocatalytic reaction system. The CQDs not only produce UV light to excite La2Ti2O7 nanosheets, but also work as electron accepters to promote the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Our work offers an easy and applicable pathway to obtain excellent photocatalysts for utilizing solar energy more efficiently.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for grant from the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Grand No. B2016202213).
